questions.text: Who is the author of 'Deep Water'?
questions.explanation: William Douglas, an advisor to President Roosevelt, wrote this autobiographical account.

questions.text: At what age did the author's aversion to water start?
questions.explanation: His aversion began when his father took him to the beach in California when he was three or four years old.

questions.text: What happened to Douglas at the California beach that created a fear of water?
options: He was bitten by a fish
A big wave knocked him over and swept over him
questions.explanation: The sheer force of the waves and being buried in water terrified the young child.

questions.text: Where did the author decide to learn swimming when he was ten or eleven?
questions.explanation: He chose the YMCA pool because it was safe, unlike the treacherous Yakima River.

questions.text: Why was the Yakima River described as 'treacherous'?
options: Because many people had drowned in it
questions.explanation: The river had a history of dangerous currents and frequent drownings.

questions.text: Describe the depth of the YMCA pool.
options: 2 feet deep at the shallow end and 9 feet at the deep end
questions.explanation: The pool had a gradual drop, making it suitable for beginners.

questions.text: What does the author refer to as the 'misadventure'?
options: The YMCA pool incident where he was thrown in
questions.explanation: The 'misadventure' refers to the time a big bully tossed him into the deep end of the pool.

questions.text: Who threw Douglas into the swimming pool?
options: A big bruiser of a boy, about eighteen
questions.explanation: A large, muscular boy picked him up and tossed him into the deep end for fun.

questions.text: What was Douglas's plan as he sank to the bottom of the pool?
options: To make a big jump when his feet hit the bottom
To wait for someone to dive in
questions.explanation: He planned to jump up to the surface, lie flat, and paddle to the edge.

questions.text: How many times did Douglas try to jump to the surface?
questions.explanation: He made three distinct attempts to jump up, but each time he failed to reach the edge and grew weaker.

questions.text: What feeling did Douglas have when he finally gave up and blacked out?
questions.explanation: He described it as a quiet, peaceful sensation, as if he were being carried in tender arms.

questions.text: What was the bully's excuse for throwing Douglas in?
options: He said, 'But I was only fooling'
He thought Douglas knew how to swim
questions.explanation: The boy didn't realize the danger and claimed it was just a joke.

questions.text: How did the incident affect Douglas's life for years to come?
options: He was haunted by the fear of water
questions.explanation: The terror stayed with him, ruining his fishing trips, canoeing, and boating activities.

questions.text: When did Douglas finally decide to get an instructor to overcome his fear?
questions.explanation: After years of being deprived of water activities, he hired an instructor in October.

questions.text: How did the instructor build a swimmer 'piece by piece'?
options: By pushing him into the lake
By teaching him sub-skills like breathing and kicking separately
questions.explanation: The instructor used a rope and pulley, and taught him breathing, leg movements, and strokes one by one.

questions.text: How long did it take the instructor to finish Douglas's training?
questions.explanation: The systematic training lasted about six months, from October until April.

questions.text: Why was Douglas still not satisfied after the instructor said he was finished?
options: He feared the old terror would return when he was alone in water
He wanted to learn more styles
questions.explanation: He needed to prove to himself that he could handle being in water without the instructor's support.

questions.text: Where did Douglas go to test his swimming skills alone?
questions.explanation: He went to Lake Wentworth, dived off a dock at Triggs Island, and swam two miles.

questions.text: What did Douglas say to the 'terror' when it returned in the middle of the lake?
options: 'Well, Mr. Terror, what do you think you can do to me?'
questions.explanation: He challenged his fear with confidence, causing the terror to flee.

questions.text: Where did Douglas have his final test to be completely sure his fear was gone?
questions.explanation: He camped by Warm Lake and swam across to the other shore and back.

questions.text: What is the deeper meaning of the quote 'All we have to fear is fear itself'?
options: It is our psychological fear that paralyzes us, not the danger
questions.explanation: The story emphasizes that terror is in the mind, and overcoming it requires strong willpower.

questions.text: Who said 'All we have to fear is fear itself'?
questions.explanation: Douglas quotes Roosevelt to summarize his experience with death and fear.

questions.text: In the context of the story, what does 'Yellow Water' symbolize?
questions.explanation: As he was drowning, everything he saw was a dirty yellow glow, representing his terror.

questions.text: What did Douglas feel in his lungs while drowning?
questions.explanation: The physical strain of holding his breath and the pressure of water made his lungs throb.

questions.text: What was the 'curtain of life' falling?
options: Losing consciousness and coming close to death
questions.explanation: The expression refers to the moment he stopped fighting and blacked out into total darkness.

questions.text: Douglas's determination to overcome fear shows his:
questions.explanation: The story is a testament to how human spirit can overcome any deep-seated phobia through effort.

questions.text: Which fishing activities was Douglas missing out on?
questions.explanation: His fear prevented him from enjoying fishing for landlocked salmon, bass, and trout.

questions.text: How did Douglas practice 'breathing' under water?
options: Holding his breath for an hour
Exhaling under water and inhaling above it
questions.explanation: The instructor taught him to put his face under water to exhale and raise his nose to inhale.

questions.text: What was the role of the 'rope' in the training?
options: To provide security and prevent drowning during practice
questions.explanation: The rope was attached to his belt and a pulley, allowing the instructor to hold him as he practiced.

questions.text: The theme of the lesson is:
options: Overcoming fear through courage and hard work
questions.explanation: The primary focus is the psychological victory over a disabling childhood fear.

questions.text: When Douglas was in the pool, the 'nine feet' felt like:
questions.explanation: To a drowning person in panic, the distance to the bottom seems much greater.

questions.text: What happened when Douglas first tried to use his legs in training?
options: They refused to work and felt paralyzed
questions.explanation: Initially, his legs were rigid with fear and wouldn't move, but eventually, they relaxed.

questions.text: The word 'bruiser' in the text refers to:
options: A person who is tough and muscular
questions.explanation: The boy who threw him in is called a bruiser because of his physical strength.

questions.text: How did the author feel after he was rescued from the pool?
options: Weak, trembling, and unable to eat
He was angry at his mother
questions.explanation: He reached home in a state of shock and physical exhaustion.

questions.text: What did Douglas do to ensure the 'residual doubts' were gone?
options: He read a book on swimming
questions.explanation: He took a final expedition to Warm Lake to confirm he was truly over his fear.

questions.text: The story 'Deep Water' is primarily a/an:
questions.explanation: It is an excerpt from his own book 'Of Men and Mountains'.

questions.text: What does the author mean by 'the terror that is in death'?
options: There is peace in death, but terror in the *fear* of dying
questions.explanation: He realized that the process of dying is peaceful; it's the anticipation and fear that is terrifying.

questions.text: Why did his mother keep warning him against the Yakima River?
options: She knew the river was dangerous and had details of drownings
questions.explanation: She acted as a reminder of the dangers to keep him safe.

questions.text: What did Douglas find 'repulsive' at the YMCA pool initially?
questions.explanation: He was self-conscious about his thin legs but subdued his pride to learn.

questions.text: The experience of overcoming fear made Douglas feel:
questions.explanation: Surviving the ordeal and conquering the fear gave him a renewed zest for living.
